notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Based on your memory that you're self-employed as a UX consultant filing Schedule C in Austin, TX with no dependents and a preference for low-admin financial systems, this is a well-timed question — and the answer depends on how much you want to maximize contributions versus keeping things simple.
For most self-employed consultants at moderate-to-high income levels, the solo 401(k) wins on contribution power, but the SEP IRA wins on simplicity and flexibility. Here's how to think through it for your specific situation.
Contribution potential: the core difference
The fundamental distinction is that a solo 401(k) lets you contribute in two roles — as both employee and employer — while a SEP IRA only allows employer contributions.
With a solo 401(k) in 2026, you can contribute:
Employee deferral: up to $24,500 (flat, not percentage-based)
Employer contribution: up to roughly 20% of your net self-employment income
Combined cap: $72,000 total (before any catch-up contributions)
With a SEP IRA in 2026, you can contribute:
Employer only: up to 25% of net self-employment compensation, capped at $72,000
No employee deferral at all
Since tax matters involve specialized expertise, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor or CPA for advice specific
